--- SOURCE 1: Partnership for Climate-Smart Commodities - Legacy Site - USDA ---
URL: https://www.usda.gov/climate-solutions/climate-smart-commodities

SUMMARY:
Through this new opportunity, USDA will finance partnerships to support the production and marketing of climate-smart commodities via a set of pilot projects lasting one to five years. On September 14, 2022, Secretary Vilsack announced USDA would be investing up to $2.8 billion in 70 selected projects under the first pool of the Partnerships for Climate-Smart Commodities funding opportunity. Proposals from **$5 million to $100 million** are in the first funding pool and should include large-scale pilot projects that emphasize the greenhouse gas benefits of climate-smart commodity production and include direct, meaningful benefits to a representative cross-section of production agriculture, including small and/or underserved producers. *   Deadline for USDA’s Partnerships for Climate-Smart Commodities Second Funding Pool is June 10 (May 26, 2022)

# Funding the Future: Evaluating public & private investments in climate-smart row crop agriculture

New Environmental Defense Fund analysis of climate-smart investments in U.S. row crops unearths strategic opportunities for the private sector to accelerate climate action and future-proof agricultural supply chains.

![](https://blogs.edf.org/wp-content/blogs.dir/90/files//2024/12/Panel1-1024x683.webp "Panel1")

As the U.S. faces potential rollbacks of public investment in climate-smart agriculture, food and agriculture companies have a critical window of opportunity to double down on strategies to fill the gap and scale private sector investments.

The urgency for investment is rapidly growing as the agriculture sector finds itself on the frontlines of a hotter planet. Extreme weather is already impacting U.S. crop output, farmer livelihoods, and the economy, with $21 billion in crop losses recorded in 2023 alone.

Environmental Defense Fund’s timely new analysis, “**Funding the Future: Evaluating public & private investments in climate-smart row crop agriculture**,” offers much-needed guidance for the private sector, highlighting strategic investment opportunities to accelerate climate action and enhance supply chain resilience in U.S. row crops.

## Case Studies

Explore all the case studies on a map

## The basics

Climate-smart agriculture (CSA) is an integrative approach to address these interlinked challenges of food security and climate change, that explicitly aims for three objectives:

A. Sustainably increasing agricultural productivity, to support equitable increases in farm incomes, food security and development;

B. Adapting and building resilience of agricultural and food security systems to climate change at multiple levels; and

C. Reducing greenhouse gas emissions from agriculture (including crops, livestock and fisheries).

## Entry points

Agriculture affects and is affected by climate change in a wide range of ways and there are numerous entry points for initiating CSA programmes or enhancing existing activities. Productivity, mitigation and adaptation actions can take place at different technological, organizational, institutional and political levels. To help you navigate these myriad entry points we have grouped them under three Thematic Areas: (i) CSA practices, (ii) CSA systems approaches, and (iii) Enabling environments for CSA. Each entry point is then described and analysed in terms of productivity, adoption and mitigation potential and is illustrated with cases studies, references and internet links for further information.

## Develop a CSA plan

Planning for, implementing and monitoring CSA projects and programmes evolves around issues of understanding the context including identification of major problems/barriers and opportunities related to the focus of the programme; developing and prioritizing solutions and designing plans; implementation; and monitoring and evaluation. Most major development agencies have their own framework for project and programme formulation and management but CCAFS has developed a specific approach for planning, implementing and assessing CSA projects and programme called CSA plan. CSA plan was developed to provide a guide for operationalizing CSA planning, implementation and monitoring at scale. CSA plan consist of four major components: (1) Situation analysis; (2) Targeting and prioritizing; (3) Program support; and (4) Monitoring. evaluation and learning.

## Finance

To meet the objectives of CSA, such as agricultural development, food security and climate change adaptation and mitigation, a number of potential funding sources are available. For instance, climate finance sources may be used to leverage agriculture finance and mainstream climate change into agricultural investments. This section offers an overview of potential sources of funding for activities in climate-smart agriculture (CSA) at national, regional and international levels and for a number of different potential ‘clients’ including governments, civil society, development organizations and others. Additionally, it includes options to search among a range of funding opportunities according to CSA focus area, sector and financing instrument.

## Resource library

CSA Guide provides a short and concise introduction and overview of the multifaceted aspects of climate-smart agriculture. At the same time it offers links to references and key resources that allows for further investigations and understanding of specific topics of interest. In the resource library we have gathered all the references, key resources, terms and questions in one place for a quick overview and easy access that can be used as a part of or independently of the other sections of the website. The resource library is divided into six sections; (1) References – list all publications, links and blogs referred to on the website; (2) Tools – list all the CSA tools presented on the website; (3) Key terms – explains the most important and frequently used terms related to CSA; (4) Frequently asked questions (FAQ) – provides a rapid overview of the most common questions asked on climate-smart agriculture; (5) About – where you can find out more about the purpose and structure of, as well as on the organizations and authors behind the website; (6) Contact.

20 Grant Opportunities for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
========================================================

Climate-resilient agriculture represents a transformative approach to farming that seeks to adapt agricultural practices to the realities of climate change while simultaneously mitigating its impacts. As global temperatures rise and weather patterns become increasingly erratic, traditional farming methods face unprecedented challenges. Droughts, floods, and shifting pest populations threaten food security and the livelihoods of farmers worldwide.

In response, climate-resilient [agriculture](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/category/agriculture-food-nutrition/ "agriculture") emphasizes sustainable practices that enhance the ability of agricultural systems to withstand these stresses. This includes diversifying crops, improving soil health, and adopting water-efficient irrigation techniques. The concept of climate-resilient agriculture is not merely a reaction to environmental changes; it is a proactive strategy that integrates scientific [research](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/category/research/ "research"), indigenous knowledge, and innovative technologies.

For instance, agroecological practices such as intercropping and cover cropping can enhance biodiversity and soil fertility, making farms more resilient to climate shocks. Additionally, the use of climate-smart technologies, such as precision agriculture tools that optimize resource use, can significantly reduce the carbon footprint of farming operations. By embracing these methods, farmers can not only safeguard their yields but also contribute to broader environmental goals, such as reducing greenhouse gas emissions and preserving natural ecosystems.

Government Grant Opportunities for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
----------------------------------------------------------------

### USDA Funding Opportunities

In the [United States](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/tag/united-states/ "United States"), for example, the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) offers several funding opportunities aimed at promoting sustainable agricultural practices.

### Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE) Program

The Sustainable Agriculture Research and Education (SARE) program provides grants for research and education projects that advance sustainable farming techniques. These grants are designed to empower farmers with the knowledge and resources needed to implement climate-resilient practices on their farms.

### Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP)

In addition to SARE, the USDA’s Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) administers the Environmental Quality Incentives Program (EQIP), which offers financial assistance to farmers who adopt conservation practices that improve soil health and water quality. This program is particularly relevant for those looking to implement climate-resilient strategies, as it encourages practices such as cover cropping, reduced tillage, and improved irrigation management. By providing financial support for these initiatives, government grants play a crucial role in facilitating the transition to more resilient agricultural systems.

Non-Profit Grant Opportunities for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
----------------------------------------------------------------

Non-profit organizations also play a vital role in funding climate-resilient agriculture initiatives. Many non-profits focus on environmental sustainability and food [security](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/category/security/ "security"), offering grants that support innovative agricultural practices. For instance, the Rodale Institute provides funding for research and education in regenerative organic agriculture, which emphasizes soil health and biodiversity as key components of resilience.

Their grant programs often target small-scale farmers who may lack access to traditional funding sources. Another notable example is the National Sustainable Agriculture Coalition (NSAC), which advocates for policies that support sustainable farming practices. NSAC offers various grant opportunities aimed at promoting climate resilience among farmers.

These grants often focus on community-based projects that empower local farmers to adopt sustainable practices while fostering collaboration among stakeholders. By leveraging funding from non-profit organizations, farmers can access resources that enable them to implement climate-resilient strategies tailored to their specific regional challenges.

Corporate Grant Opportunities for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
---------------------------------------------------------------

Corporate entities are increasingly recognizing the importance of sustainability in agriculture and are stepping up to provide grant opportunities for climate-resilient initiatives. Many corporations have established foundations or corporate social responsibility (CSR) programs that focus on environmental stewardship and sustainable agriculture. For example, the Bayer Fund offers grants through its “Grow Community” program, which supports projects that promote sustainable farming practices and enhance community resilience.

![Image 3: Thousands of Grants - Premium Access](https://files.elfsightcdn.com/eafe4a4d-3436-495d-b748-5bdce62d911d/83ad8b55-f5c7-4512-a323-9cb2ea620cbe/Thousands-of-Grants-Premium-Access.png)

Additionally, companies in the agricultural technology sector are investing in research and [development](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/category/development/ "development") aimed at creating innovative solutions for climate-resilient agriculture. These corporations often provide funding for pilot projects that test new technologies or practices in real-world settings. For instance, companies like Cargill and Syngenta have launched initiatives that fund research into precision agriculture tools designed to optimize resource use and minimize environmental impact.

By collaborating with farmers and researchers, these corporations not only contribute to climate resilience but also position themselves as leaders in sustainable agriculture.

International Grant Opportunities for Climate-Resilient Agriculture
-------------------------------------------------------------------

On a global scale, numerous international organizations offer grant opportunities aimed at promoting climate-resilient agriculture in developing countries. The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) of the United Nations provides funding for projects that enhance food security and resilience in the face of climate change. Their initiatives often focus on capacity building, helping farmers adopt sustainable practices that improve productivity while conserving natural resources.

![Image 4: Thousands of Grants - Premium Access](https://files.elfsightcdn.com/eafe4a4d-3436-495d-b748-5bdce62d911d/83ad8b55-f5c7-4512-a323-9cb2ea620cbe/Thousands-of-Grants-Premium-Access.png)

Another significant player in this arena is the Global Environment Facility (GEF), which funds projects that address environmental challenges while promoting [sustainable development](https://www2.fundsforngos.org/category/sustainable-development/ "sustainable development"). GEF has supported numerous initiatives aimed at enhancing agricultural resilience through innovative practices such as agroforestry and integrated pest management. By providing financial resources to countries facing severe climate impacts, these international grants help build local capacity and foster long-term sustainability in agricultural systems.

Tips for Applying for Climate-Resilient Agriculture Grants
----------------------------------------------------------

Navigating the landscape of grant opportunities for climate-resilient agriculture can be daunting, but there are several strategies that can enhance the likelihood of success in securing funding. First and foremost, it is essential to thoroughly research available grants and understand their specific requirements. Each grant program has its own set of eligibility criteria, application processes, and deadlines.

Familiarizing oneself with these details can save time and effort in the long run. When preparing an application, it is crucial to articulate a clear project proposal that outlines the objectives, methodologies, and expected outcomes of the initiative. Providing concrete data and evidence to support claims can significantly strengthen an application.

![Image 5: Thousands of Grants - Premium Access](https://files.elfsightcdn.com/eafe4a4d-3436-495d-b748-5bdce62d911d/83ad8b55-f5c7-4512-a323-9cb2ea620cbe/Thousands-of-Grants-Premium-Access.png)

For instance, including case studies or examples of similar successful projects can demonstrate feasibility and potential impact. Additionally, engaging with local stakeholders and building partnerships can enhance credibility and show a collaborative approach to addressing climate resilience. Furthermore, applicants should pay close attention to budgetary requirements and ensure that their proposed budget aligns with the funding guidelines of the grant program.

A well-structured budget that clearly outlines how funds will be allocated can instill confidence in reviewers regarding the project’s viability. Lastly, seeking feedback from peers or mentors before submission can provide valuable insights and help refine the proposal. In conclusion, as climate change continues to pose significant challenges to global agriculture, securing funding through various grant opportunities becomes increasingly vital for farmers looking to adopt climate-resilient practices.

By leveraging government, non-profit, corporate, and international grants, agricultural stakeholders can access the resources necessary to innovate and adapt their operations for a more sustainable future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Significant investment is essential for advancing climate-smart crop breeding research, ensuring effective adaptation strategies against climate change impacts on agriculture.
Partnering with universities enhances research capabilities, fostering innovation and knowledge transfer necessary for developing resilient agricultural practices.
Collaboration between government and private sectors is vital to leverage resources, enhance technology access, and implement large-scale climate-smart agriculture initiatives.
